question_text: A student compares three scenarios for lifting a 5 kg box 2 metres up a slope. Scenario 1: Using an electric motor (90% efficient). Scenario 2: Using a hand crank with pulleys (60% efficient). Scenario 3: Carrying it up manually (40% efficient). For each scenario, the gravitational potential energy gained is 100 J. Which scenario requires the most input energy from an energy store, and why do the efficiencies differ?

options.label: A
options.text: Scenario 1 requires most input energy because motors are more powerful and waste more energy overall.

options.label: B
options.text: Scenario 3 requires most input energy (250 J) because the manual system is least efficient; more chemical energy from muscles is wasted as thermal energy and sound.

options.label: C
options.text: Scenario 2 requires most input energy because pulleys create friction and reduce efficiency.

options.label: D
options.text: All scenarios require the same input energy because the gravitational potential energy gained is identical in each case.

explanation: Efficiency tells us what fraction of input energy becomes useful energy. To find input energy, divide useful energy by efficiency: 100 J ÷ 0.40 = 250 J (manual), 100 J ÷ 0.60 = 167 J (pulley), 100 J ÷ 0.90 = 111 J (motor). The least efficient system wastes the most energy as heat, so the chemical energy store in muscles must supply 250 J even though only 100 J becomes gravitational potential energy.
